courseTitle: AutoCAD Setup Guide for Municipal Residential Drawing
courseDescription: A complete beginner-friendly guide covering all essential AutoCAD setup steps required before starting any municipal or residential drawing. Written in simple, professional English for students and beginner engineers.
courseObjectives: Learn how to properly set up AutoCAD before starting any drawing
Understand units, layers, text styles, and dimension styles
Create professional and organized drawings ready for municipal submission
Save a reusable template to speed up all future projects

phases.phaseTitle: Open AutoCAD and Create Your File

steps.title: Open AutoCAD and Select the Correct Workspace
steps.subtitle: Drafting & Annotation workspace
steps.why: AutoCAD has several workspaces. For all municipal and residential drawing work, the Drafting & Annotation workspace gives you the right tools in the right places.
instructions: Open AutoCAD from your desktop or Start Menu
Wait for the software to fully load before doing anything
Find the Workspace Switcher dropdown at the top of the screen
Select: Drafting & Annotation
The ribbon will now show all drawing and annotation tools correctly

note.label: Why this workspace?
note.text: Drafting & Annotation workspace shows the Annotate tab, dimension tools, and text tools which are all essential for professional municipal drawings.

steps.title: Create a New Drawing Using the Metric Template
steps.subtitle: Use acadiso.dwt — not acad.dwt
steps.why: The template you choose at the start sets your unit system. The wrong template means wrong units, wrong dimensions, and completely wasted work.
instructions: Press Ctrl+N — the Select Template dialog opens
Find acadiso.dwt in the list and select it
Click Open — your new blank metric drawing is ready
acadiso.dwt is the ISO Metric template — it uses millimeters by default
Never use acad.dwt — that template uses inches and will cause wrong dimensions

note.label: Common beginner mistake
note.text: Many beginners accidentally use acad.dwt which uses inches. Always confirm you selected acadiso.dwt before starting any work.

steps.title: Save the File Immediately With a Proper Name
steps.subtitle: Save before drawing a single line
steps.why: If AutoCAD crashes before you save, all your work is permanently lost. Always save first, then start drawing.
instructions: Press Ctrl+S — the Save As dialog opens
Create a dedicated project folder (example: Project_ResidentialBuilding_2081)
Name the file clearly, for example: FloorPlan_GroundFloor_v1.dwg
Click Save to confirm
Press Ctrl+S every 10 minutes while working to prevent data loss

note.label: Good file naming habit
note.text: Use descriptive names like SitePlan_v1.dwg or Elevation_North_v2.dwg. Avoid generic names like Drawing1.dwg or Untitled.dwg — they become confusing very quickly.

steps.title: Set Drawing Units to Millimeters
steps.subtitle: The single most important setup step
steps.why: If units are set incorrectly, every dimension, block, and hatch will be wrong. This must be correctly configured before drawing anything at all.
instructions: Type UN and press Enter — the Drawing Units dialog opens
Length Type: select Decimal
Precision: select 0 for whole numbers, or 0.00 if decimal precision is needed
Insertion Scale: select Millimeters — this is the critical setting
Angle Type: Decimal Degrees is correct for standard drawings
Click OK to confirm all settings

note.label: Do not skip this step
note.text: Insertion Scale set to Millimeters ensures that when you insert doors, windows, or furniture blocks, they appear at the correct real-world size. Skipping this causes all blocks to insert at the wrong scale.

steps.title: Enable Essential Drafting Tools from the Status Bar
steps.subtitle: Ortho, Object Snap, Polar Tracking, Dynamic Input
steps.why: These four tools are the foundation of accurate drafting. Without them, lines will be crooked, walls will not connect properly, and placing objects accurately becomes very difficult.
instructions: F8 — Turn ON Ortho Mode: locks the cursor to perfectly straight horizontal and vertical lines only
F3 — Turn ON Object Snap: snaps the cursor exactly to endpoints, midpoints, and centers of existing objects
F10 — Turn ON Polar Tracking: automatically guides the cursor at 45° and 90° angles
F12 — Turn ON Dynamic Input: lets you type exact dimensions directly at the cursor position
Check the status bar at the bottom — all four icons should appear active and highlighted

note.label: Quick visual check
note.text: Look at the status bar at the very bottom of AutoCAD. The Ortho, Osnap, Polar, and DYN icons should all appear active (colored), not grayed out.

steps.title: Configure Object Snap Settings
steps.subtitle: Choose which snap points AutoCAD will use
steps.why: Object Snap tells AutoCAD which exact points to snap to. Too few snaps cause walls to miss connections. Too many snaps cause the cursor to jump to unwanted points.
instructions: Type OS and press Enter — the Drafting Settings dialog opens
Go to the Object Snap tab
Enable only these six snaps: Endpoint, Midpoint, Center, Intersection, Perpendicular, Nearest
Disable all other snaps to keep snapping clean and predictable
Click OK to confirm

note.label: Do not enable all snaps
note.text: Enabling every snap option makes the cursor jump unpredictably to random points. Use only the six listed above for reliable, accurate snapping.

steps.title: Set Display Background and Crosshair Size
steps.subtitle: Comfortable and clear drawing environment
steps.why: A dark background reduces eye strain during long drawing sessions. A larger crosshair helps you stay oriented inside large municipal drawings.
instructions: Type OP and press Enter — the Options dialog opens
Go to the Display tab
Click the Colors button → Drawing Window Background → select Black or Dark Gray
Click Apply & Close
Back in Display tab, find Crosshair Size — change from 5 to 15 or 20
Click Apply then OK to save all display changes

note.label: Does background color affect printing?
note.text: No. The background color is only for your screen comfort. It has absolutely no effect on how the drawing prints or plots on paper.

steps.title: Open Layer Properties Manager
steps.subtitle: Your drawing's organization system
steps.why: Layers separate different parts of your drawing — walls, dimensions, text, doors — into organized groups. This makes editing, printing, and reviewing drawings much easier and more professional.
instructions: Type LA and press Enter — the Layer Properties Manager panel opens
This panel shows all layers currently in your drawing
The default layer is called 0 — never draw your actual work on layer 0
You will create new dedicated layers for each type of drawing element
Keep this panel open while creating all your layers

note.label: Think of layers like folders
note.text: Just like you organize different files into different folders on your computer, you organize different drawing elements onto different layers in AutoCAD.

steps.title: Create Standard Layers for Municipal Drawings
steps.subtitle: One layer per drawing element type
steps.why: Professional municipal drawings require properly organized layers. This makes it easy to control visibility, print line weights, and edit specific elements without affecting others.
instructions: Click the New Layer button (page icon at top of Layer panel) to create each layer
Create all layers from the standard layer table below
Assign the correct color, linetype, and lineweight to each layer as listed
For CENTER2 linetype: if it is missing, click the linetype cell and click Load to find it
Do not rename or skip any layer — all are needed for complete municipal drawings

standardLayers.name: TEXT
standardLayers.use: Room names, notes, and all text labels

standardLayers.name: CENTERLINE
standardLayers.use: Column grid lines and center references

standardLayers.name: DOOR
standardLayers.use: Door openings and door swing arcs

note.label: Linetype note
note.text: If CENTER2 linetype is not available, click the linetype cell for the CENTERLINE layer, click Load, find CENTER2 in the list, and click OK to load it.

steps.title: Always Set the Correct Current Layer Before Drawing
steps.subtitle: Check this before placing any object
steps.why: Whatever layer is set as current is where all new objects will be placed. Drawing walls on the TEXT layer or dimensions on the WALL layer creates a disorganized drawing that is very difficult to fix later.
instructions: Look at the Layer dropdown in the ribbon at the top left of the screen
It shows which layer is currently active
Before drawing walls — set current layer to WALL
Before adding dimensions — set current layer to DIMENSION
Before typing notes — set current layer to TEXT
Make checking the current layer a habit before starting any new task

note.label: Most common beginner mistake
note.text: Drawing everything on a single layer (usually Layer 0) makes it impossible to control visibility, printing, and editing later. Always place objects on the correct layer.

phases.phaseTitle: Text Style and Dimension Style Setup

steps.title: Create a Professional Text Style
steps.subtitle: STYLE command — set font and width factor
steps.why: AutoCAD's default font looks unprofessional on printed municipal drawings. Setting up a proper text style makes all your text clean, readable, and consistent.
instructions: Type ST and press Enter — the Text Style dialog opens
Click New and name it: MUNICIPAL_TEXT
Font Name: select romans.shx — a clean, professional, standard engineering font
Height: leave it at 0 so you can set the height manually each time you place text
Width Factor: type 0.85 — slightly condensed text saves space on drawings
Click Apply then Close

note.label: Why leave height at 0?
note.text: If you set a fixed height in the text style, you cannot change the size when placing individual text objects. Leaving height at 0 gives you full flexibility to choose the right size each time.

steps.title: Create a Dimension Style for Your Drawing Scale
steps.subtitle: DIMSTYLE — control arrows, text, and dimension lines
steps.why: Without a proper dimension style, arrows may be too large, text too small, or dimension lines too close together. The style must match your intended print scale to look correct on paper.
instructions: Type D and press Enter — the Dimension Style Manager opens
Click New and name it: DIM_100 (for 1:100 scale drawings)
Lines tab: Extension line offset from origin = 2, Baseline spacing = 8
Symbols and Arrows tab: Arrowhead = Architectural Tick, Arrow size = 2.5
Text tab: Text style = MUNICIPAL_TEXT, Text height = 2.5
Primary Units tab: Unit format = Decimal, Precision = 0
Click OK, then click Set Current, then click Close

note.label: Different scale needs a different style
note.text: For 1:50 scale drawings, create a separate style called DIM_50 with smaller values (arrow size 1.5, text height 2.0). Each different print scale requires its own properly configured dimension style.

steps.title: Set the Annotation Scale
steps.subtitle: Links text and dimension sizes to the print scale
steps.why: Annotation scale connects your text and dimension sizes to the drawing's print scale. If set incorrectly, text and arrows will appear too large or too small when the drawing is printed.
instructions: Look at the status bar at the bottom-right of the screen
Find the Annotation Scale dropdown — it shows 1:1 by default
Click it and select your drawing's intended print scale
Floor plans: use 1:100
Site plans: use 1:200 or 1:500
Detail drawings: use 1:20 or 1:50

note.label: What this controls
note.text: At annotation scale 1:100, text set to 2.5 mm height will print exactly 2.5 mm on paper. This keeps all text and dimensions at a readable and professional size regardless of drawing scale.

phases.phaseTitle: Final Check and Save as Template

steps.title: Run a Final Setup Checklist Before Drawing
steps.subtitle: Two minutes now saves hours of rework later
steps.why: A quick verification before starting drawing prevents the most common setup errors — especially wrong units and missing layers — which are very difficult and time-consuming to fix later.
instructions: Type UN — confirm: Length = Decimal, Insertion Scale = Millimeters
Type LA — confirm: all required layers exist with correct colors and linetypes
Type D — confirm: dimension style DIM_100 is created and set as current
Type ST — confirm: MUNICIPAL_TEXT style is created
Check status bar: Ortho (F8), Osnap (F3), Polar (F10), Dynamic Input (F12) are all ON
Check the Layer dropdown: correct current layer is selected before starting
finalChecklist: All standard layers created with correct colors and linetypes
Dimension style DIM_100 created and set as current
Ortho, Object Snap, Polar Tracking, Dynamic Input are all active

note.label: Ready to draw
note.text: If all checklist items are confirmed, your AutoCAD environment is fully prepared and professional. You can now begin drawing walls, grids, or the site boundary with confidence.

steps.title: Save Your Setup as a Reusable Drawing Template
steps.subtitle: Do this once — use it for every future project
steps.why: A template saves all your layers, text styles, dimension styles, and settings permanently. Every future project starts fully prepared without repeating any of these setup steps.
instructions: Go to File menu and click Save As
Change the file type from .dwg to AutoCAD Drawing Template (*.dwt)
Name the file: Municipal_Residential_Template.dwt
Save it in AutoCAD's default Templates folder so it appears in the new drawing dialog
From now on: File > New > select Municipal_Residential_Template.dwt and all setup is instantly ready

note.label: Professional time-saving habit
note.text: Every professional engineer and draftsman works from a standard template. This single step saves 20 to 30 minutes at the start of every new project.
